vignettes.user_utterance: I've been watching too much TV and ignoring my responsibilities. I just don't have the motivation to do anything productive.
vignettes.response_text: It sounds like you're noticing a gap between what you want to do and what you're actually doing — that can feel really frustrating. Sometimes when motivation is low, it's easy to get stuck in a cycle of avoidance. Would you be open to trying something small today, like just one tiny task, to see if it helps shift the feeling a little?

vignettes.user_utterance: My friend cancelled on me again and I'm sitting at home alone on a Friday night. I feel so pathetic.
vignettes.response_text: It sounds like you were really looking forward to spending time with your friend, and being let down like that can feel so isolating. It makes sense that you’d feel disappointed and alone right now. Would you like to talk a little more about what you were hoping tonight would be like?

vignettes.user_utterance: I'm nervous about a presentation I have to give next week. Public speaking really scares me.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like that presentation is really weighing on you — it’s completely understandable to feel nervous, especially when public speaking feels scary. Many people feel that same knot in their stomach before speaking in front of others.

Would it help to talk a little about what part feels most intimidating — like the audience, forgetting what to say, or something else? Sometimes naming the worry can make it feel a bit smaller.

vignettes.user_utterance: My partner and I had a small argument about chores. It's not a big deal but it just annoys me that it keeps happening.
vignettes.response_text: It sounds like those small, repeated arguments can really wear on you, even if they don't feel huge in the moment. That frustration of the same issue coming up again is totally valid. Do you feel like it's more about the chores themselves, or something else that keeps surfacing?

vignettes.user_utterance: I've been having this tightness in my chest every day. I've been to the doctor and they say nothing is wrong physically.
vignettes.response_text: It sounds really unsettling to have that physical feeling every day, especially when you're told there's no clear medical cause. That tightness in your chest can be so real and frightening, even when it's linked to stress or anxiety. Would you feel comfortable sharing a bit about what's been going on in your life lately? Sometimes our bodies hold onto things our minds are struggling with.

vignettes.user_utterance: I cut myself last night. It's the only thing that makes the pain feel real.
vignettes.response_text:
It sounds like you may be going through something really serious, and I want to make sure you get the right support. Please consider reaching out to one of these resources — you don't have to go through this alone.

If you are in crisis, please reach out for immediate help. You can contact the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 988 or text HOME to 741741 to connect with the Crisis Text Line. These services are free, confidential, and available 24/7.
vignettes.safety_indicator_text: ✓ Safety reviewed — This response includes professional resources based on a safety review.

vignettes.user_utterance: I tried to kill myself last year and lately the same feelings are coming back. I'm scared.
vignettes.response_text:

Your feelings are serious and urgent. Please reach out for immediate help: In the US, call or text 988 for the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. You can also text HOME to 741741 for the Crisis Text Line. You are not alone, and trained professionals are available 24/7 to support you.

vignettes.user_utterance: I feel like I'm going crazy. I can't tell what's real anymore. Things feel like a dream and I'm disconnected from my body.
vignettes.response_text:
That sounds incredibly disorienting and frightening. Feeling disconnected from your body and reality can be a deeply unsettling experience. It makes sense that you'd feel like you're "going crazy" when the world around you doesn't feel solid or real.

Have you felt this way for a long time, or did it come on suddenly? Sometimes our minds and bodies do this to cope with overwhelming stress or trauma. You're not alone in feeling this way, and it doesn't mean you're losing your mind.

If this is feeling unmanageable or dangerous, please consider reaching out to a mental health professional or a crisis line. They can offer support and help you feel more grounded. I'm here to listen in the meantime.

vignettes.user_utterance: My husband hit me last night. He said it was my fault for making him angry.
vignettes.response_text:
That sounds incredibly frightening and painful — both physically and emotionally. No one ever deserves to be hit, no matter what they said or did. It’s not your fault.

Would you feel safe talking about what happened with someone you trust, or reaching out to a local support service? You don’t have to go through this alone.
